metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the '(name it)' placeholder with the actual function/module path that prunes REVIEW_RESULT events and explicitly state that TICKET_DIGEST is registered in the same prune list (e.g., add it alongside REVIEW_RESULT in that path).
findings.finding: The prune/retire path for TICKET_DIGEST is not actually named; the plan text contains an inline placeholder ('name it') that was never resolved, leaving the retirement lifecycle underspecified.
findings.location: Scope section, Prune/retire paragraph: 'dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and name the actual cleanup path for REVIEW_RESULT sidecars on deleted/archived tickets (or acknowledge none exists): search `delete.py`, `purge_bridge.py`, and the git-rm prune flow for any archive-triggered sidecar removal; if none exists, either add a delete hook or explicitly scope out archived-ticket cleanup with justification.
findings.finding: The plan says a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is 'dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)' — but the actual REVIEW_RESULT prune path (`sidecar.prune()` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`) only prunes by retention count (RETAIN_PER_TICKET) triggered after each emit; it does NOT specifically handle deleted/archived tickets. No evidence of a path that drops REVIEW_RESULT sidecars when a ticket is deleted/archived was found in the codebase (delete.py, purge_bridge.py, compact.py do not reference sidecar cleanup). The '(name it)' parenthetical in the plan text is an unfilled placeholder, not a grounded path.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Prune/retire: a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the prune-on-delete/archive mechanism: either (a) explicitly state that `TICKET_DIGEST` files are NOT proactively pruned on delete (acceptable for a sidecar, consistent with how REVIEW_RESULT files actually behave today — they are NOT removed on ticket delete either), or (b) scope the new on-delete hook explicitly in this plan rather than referencing a path that doesn't exist.
findings.finding: The plan directs the implementer to reuse 'the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses' for dropping digests of deleted/archived tickets — but that prune path does not exist. The actual REVIEW_RESULT `prune()` function in `sidecar.py` is a *retention* prune (keep last N per ticket), called immediately after an emit. The `delete.py` command never invokes any sidecar prune; it calls `scratch.cleanup_for_ticket` and `batch_close_operations` but nothing that removes REVIEW_RESULT or similar sidecar files from a deleted ticket's directory. Implementing this AC as written will fail silently (the digest files remain in the ticket dir indefinitely after delete/archive) or force the implementer to invent new infrastructure not described in the plan.
findings.location: Prune/retire section; AC line 'an archived/deleted ticket's digest is pruned'; Testing line 'archived ticket's digest pruned'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses' with an accurate description: REVIEW_RESULT does NOT have an on-delete prune; its files persist in deleted ticket directories. For TICKET_DIGEST, either accept the same posture (no on-delete prune, only the count-bounding retention prune after each emit) and remove the AC line, OR explicitly scope the new on-delete cleanup hook in this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and add to the edit-target list the concrete trigger that prunes TICKET_DIGEST on archive/delete (e.g. a hook in `leaf.archive` or `delete.py`, or acknowledge that prune fires only on re-enrich and scope AC5 accordingly).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)', but REVIEW_RESULT's prune() (sidecar.py:67-110) is a count-based retention prune called only post-emit — it is never triggered by archive or delete events. No archive/delete hook calls it. The plan's edit-target list includes no modification to delete.py or leaf.archive to trigger TICKET_DIGEST pruning, and AC5 ('archived/deleted ticket's digest pruned') is untestable without a trigger mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prune/retire' paragraph; AC line 5 ('archived/deleted ticket's digest pruned')
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add `src/rebar/_commands/delete.py` and `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` to the Scope edit-targets with a call to the TICKET_DIGEST prune helper after the ARCHIVED/STATUS(deleted) event is written, mirroring how REVIEW_RESULT's emit() calls prune(); or (b) narrow AC5 to 'on re-enrichment of a deleted ticket, no new digest is written' (fail-open is acceptable) and drop the prune-on-delete claim — matching the actual REVIEW_RESULT behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `stage_and_commit` hand-spelled error string (event_append.py:98–103) as an explicit edit target: `TICKET_DIGEST` must appear in that literal alongside the EVENT_TYPES frozenset addition, or the existing `test_event_types_all_listed_in_error_string` drift guard will fail.
findings.finding: The plan names `EVENT_TYPES` in `event_append.py` as the only required edit, but `stage_and_commit` also contains a hand-spelled literal error string (lines 98–103) that enumerates every `EVENT_TYPES` member. A drift-guard test (`tests/unit/test_store.py::test_event_types_all_listed_in_error_string`) asserts that every name in `EVENT_TYPES` appears in that string. Adding `TICKET_DIGEST` to `EVENT_TYPES` without also adding it to the error string literal will break this existing test — an unenumerated edit target.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` edit target; also `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py:98-103` (error string) and `tests/unit/test_store.py:76-83` (drift guard)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, extend the `event_append.py` bullet to read: 'add `"TICKET_DIGEST"` to the `EVENT_TYPES` frozenset (line ~38) **and** to the hand-spelled error string inside `stage_and_commit` (lines ~98–103) that the drift-guard test `test_event_types_all_listed_in_error_string` validates.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `test_freshness_version_mismatch` test (or extend `test_freshness_flips`) that stores a digest with an old `model` tag and/or an old `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION`, then asserts the freshness check returns NOT-current after the version is bumped.
findings.finding: The freshness check explicitly depends on BOTH `content_hash` AND `model`/`schema` version tags matching, but the only freshness test listed (`test_freshness_flips`) covers content-hash mutation only. There is no test that a stored digest carrying an old `model` or `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` tag is read as stale when the current model/version differs — leaving the version-mismatch invalidation path untested.
findings.location: Testing section / AC line: `::test_freshness_flips`; plan prose: 'digest is CURRENT iff its stored `content_hash` (and `model`/`schema` version tags) match'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_ticket_digest_store.py`, add a parametrized case: write a `TICKET_DIGEST` event with `model='gpt-4o'` and `digest_hash_version=1`, then read freshness against `model='gpt-4o-mini'` and `digest_hash_version=2`; assert result is stale. Mirror the existing `test_freshness_flips` structure but vary the version/model fields instead of ticket content.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION`: name the owning module, the exact field name stamped in the TICKET_DIGEST payload (e.g. `digest_hash_version: int`), and the freshness comparison rule; add a test for version-mismatch invalidation to the AC.
findings.finding: The `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` constant — described as the mechanism to trigger deterministic cache invalidation when `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` changes — has no specified placement: the plan does not name which module owns it, how it is stamped into the stored event, or how the freshness check reads and compares it.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Bump a `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` when the field set changes so all cached digests invalidate deterministically'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add named AC tests for model-change and schema/DIGEST_HASH_VERSION-change staleness (e.g. `::test_freshness_flips_on_model_change`, `::test_freshness_flips_on_version_bump`) so all three freshness axes have verified coverage.
findings.finding: The freshness rule references `model` and `schema` version tags as staleness triggers alongside `content_hash`, but the only staleness test in the AC (`test_freshness_flips`) covers content-hash mutation only. Model or schema version changes that should mark the digest stale have no named test coverage.
findings.location: What section — 'the digest is CURRENT iff its stored content_hash (and model/schema version tags) match'; AC — '::test_freshness_flips'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (and corresponding unit test case) covering DIGEST_HASH_VERSION-triggered staleness: mutating DIGEST_HASH_VERSION (or adding a field to DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS) must cause all previously-current digests to read as stale.
findings.finding: The `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` bump mechanism (described in Scope as a deliverable: 'Bump a `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` when the field set changes so all cached digests invalidate deterministically') has no covering acceptance criterion — it is testable behavior that can regress silently.
findings.location: Scope section (DIGEST_HASH_VERSION paragraph); Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` increment marks all prior digests stale; verified by `::test_version_bump_invalidates`.' Add the test case to `tests/unit/test_ticket_digest_store.py`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `TICKET_DIGEST` sidecar event payload schema: enumerate all field names/types (including the `content_hash`, `model`, `schema` sentinel string, and `digest` body), name the sentinel literal (e.g. `'ticket_digest_v1'`), and add it to the Scope edit targets.
findings.finding: The `TICKET_DIGEST` event payload schema — i.e. the named field vocabulary (field names, types, required fields, and the `schema` sentinel string analogous to `'plan_review_result_v1'`) stored in the event's `data` key — is never formally defined in the plan. The reader's freshness check and schema guard operate on these fields, but an executor has no authoritative field list to implement against.
findings.location: Scope section — 'A scan/read helper (mirror how REVIEW_RESULT sidecars are read)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `build_payload()` equivalent for TICKET_DIGEST to the Scope, declaring at minimum: `{"schema": "ticket_digest_v1", "content_hash": str, "model": str, "digest_hash_version": int, "digest": <ticket_digest fields from S1 schema>}`. Name the `'ticket_digest_v1'` sentinel in the plan so the reader's schema guard is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `show` output for the absent/never-enriched case (e.g., display 'no digest — run enrichment') so all three states (absent, stale, current) have defined representations.
findings.finding: `show` rendering is specified only for 'digest presence + freshness' but the display for a ticket that has never been enriched (no TICKET_DIGEST event exists at all) is not described, leaving the absent/never-computed state unhandled in the UI.
findings.location: Scope section: '`show` renders digest presence + freshness only'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third display state: when no TICKET_DIGEST event exists, show a message such as `digest: none (not yet computed)` distinct from the stale case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace '(name it)' with the actual prune path/function used by REVIEW_RESULT (e.g. `store.prune_events_for_ticket()` or equivalent) so the executor has a concrete integration point.
findings.finding: The prune/retire section contains a literal placeholder instruction — 'name it' — that defers identifying the actual prune path to the executor, rather than naming the path in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section, prune/retire bullet: 'a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Identify and state the exact prune hook — e.g. 'dropped by `_store/prune.py::prune_ticket_events`, the same path used for REVIEW_RESULT events' — removing the placeholder.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace '(name it)' with the concrete prune mechanism: name the module/function (e.g. `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune()` pattern adapted to `TICKET_DIGEST`) and the trigger point (post-archive event, or a standalone prune call) so the executor has a complete spec.
findings.finding: The prune/retire section contains a literal deferred-to-executor instruction — '(name it)' — rather than naming the concrete prune path. This is a pink-elephant antipattern: the plan requires the executor to discover and specify a design decision that should be in the plan itself.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prune/retire: a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(name it)' with: 'by a `prune_digest(ticket_id, keep=N)` function in the new sidecar module, mirroring `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune()`: `git rm` the oldest TICKET_DIGEST files beyond the retention bound and commit. Called after each write and on the archive path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific prune path/function that REVIEW_RESULT uses (e.g., the function name and file) in the Scope section, so the implementer knows exactly where to add TICKET_DIGEST pruning support.
findings.finding: Scope instructs '(name it)' for the prune path ('dropped by the same prune path REVIEW_RESULT uses (name it)') but no section of the plan ever names the specific prune path, creating a cross-section gap between the Scope's stated obligation and its fulfillment.
findings.location: Scope section (Prune/retire paragraph); no other section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope Prune/retire sentence, replace '(name it)' with the actual function name, e.g., '(the `prune_ticket_events` function in `src/rebar/_store/prune.py`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the post-rollback schema state: specify that a rollback to a pre-change binary leaves TICKET_DIGEST files on disk (DATA LOSS RISK: none, but fsck WARNs will fire), and prescribe the compensating-forward resolution (re-deploy; do not delete sidecar files); also specify that `TICKET_DIGEST` must be added to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` and shipped to all clones before any client writes a TICKET_DIGEST event.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the post-rollback schema state. If a deployment is rolled back to a binary that predates this change, TICKET_DIGEST event files remain on disk but `TICKET_DIGEST` is absent from that binary's `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`, causing `is_unknown_newer_type` to return True and fsck to emit WARN — the opposite of what AC3 asserts ('an older binary preserves-and-ignores it with no fsck WARN'). The plan must specify: (1) deploy ordering (add `TICKET_DIGEST` to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` and ship that binary to all clones BEFORE any client writes a TICKET_DIGEST event), and (2) the explicit post-rollback state (lingering TICKET_DIGEST files will trip fsck on the pre-change binary; compensating-forward resolution is to leave events in place and re-deploy).
findings.location: Prune/retire section; AC line 3 ('an older binary preserves-and-ignores it with no fsck WARN')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollout ordering' note under Scope or Prune/retire: 'Ship the binary with TICKET_DIGEST in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES to ALL clones before enabling any client to call the write path. On rollback to a pre-change binary: TICKET_DIGEST files remain on disk and will trip fsck is_unknown_newer_type (fsck WARN, not error); compensating-forward resolution is to re-deploy the new binary — do not delete sidecar files. There is no data loss on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC3 into two sub-criteria and add a fixture description for the older-binary test: e.g. 'construct a reducer instance with TICKET_DIGEST absent from KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, replay a stream containing a TICKET_DIGEST event, assert the event is preserved in output and no WARN is logged.'
findings.finding: AC3 bundles two distinct verifiable claims — (a) event-type registration in two registries, and (b) 'older binary preserves-and-ignores with no fsck WARN' — without specifying how the unit test exercises the older-binary scenario; a unit test that only checks registry membership does not prove binary-compatibility behavior, creating a fixture-invalidity risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'TICKET_DIGEST is in EVENT_TYPES and _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (not KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES); an older binary preserves-and-ignores it with no fsck WARN.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that `show` displays digest presence and freshness indicator but not the raw digest text (e.g., verified by a unit or integration test on the show formatter).
findings.finding: The `show` rendering deliverable ('show renders digest presence + freshness only') is described in Scope but has no covering acceptance criterion, leaving the UI contract unverifiable.
findings.location: Scope section ('show renders digest presence + freshness only'); Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the fail-closed freshness analogy: replace 'mirroring `compute_validity` returning None on read error' with the correct precedent — `current_material_fingerprint` returns `None` on read error, and `compute_validity` treats that as `{valid: False}` (fail-closed via dict result, not None).
findings.finding: The plan cites 'compute_validity returning None on read error' as the named precedent for fail-closed freshness, but compute_validity never returns None — it always returns a dict {valid: bool, reason: str}. The None-returning function is current_material_fingerprint(), which compute_validity calls and treats as fail-closed by returning {valid: False, reason: 'could not recompute...'}. The cited precedent is factually wrong.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Fail-closed freshness: if the current content-hash cannot be computed (partial read), the digest reads as NOT-current (never falsely fresh) — mirroring compute_validity returning None on read error.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'mirroring `compute_validity` returning None on read error' → 'mirroring `current_material_fingerprint` returning None on read error (which `compute_validity` treats as `valid=False`); the new freshness helper should similarly return a result that callers treat as NOT-current when the hash cannot be computed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the title (and any references) to clarify that the content_hash is a *freshness-check payload field*, not a storage key — the record is keyed by ticket identity and resolved by latest-HLC, e.g. 'Persist ticket digest as a latest-wins sidecar event with content-hash freshness (shared cache)'.
findings.finding: The title says 'content-hash-keyed' (implying the content-hash IS the storage key / lookup key), but the body stores one TICKET_DIGEST per ticket keyed only by ticket identity, using latest-wins-by-HLC; the content_hash is a payload field used for post-retrieval freshness comparison, not a storage key. An implementor reading the title could design a content-addressable index or multi-record-per-hash store rather than the single-latest sidecar the body actually specifies.
findings.location: Plan title vs. 'What' section and AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'Persist ticket digest as a latest-wins sidecar event with content-hash freshness check (shared cache)' — this matches the body's actual mechanism (HLC-ordered latest-wins + payload content_hash for staleness detection).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the initial value of `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` in the plan (e.g. `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION = 1`) so the executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The initial value of `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` is not stated; the plan says 'Bump a DIGEST_HASH_VERSION when the field set changes' but never declares what version 1 (or 0) is, leaving the executor to choose arbitrarily.
findings.location: Scope section, scan/read helper bullet: 'Bump a `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` when the field set changes so all cached digests invalidate deterministically.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Initial value: `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION = 1`' to the scan/read helper bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the sidecar-event pattern (preserved-and-ignored registration, content-hash freshness-on-read, HLC latest-wins, fail-closed semantics) so future LLM-artifact sidecar types have a canonical reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a second sidecar event type establishing a 'freshness-on-read content-hash' pattern for LLM artifacts — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc artifact to capture it. Future contributors adding a third sidecar will have no canonical reference for the pattern's invariants (preserved-and-ignored, content-hash freshness, HLC latest-wins, fail-closed).
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Why / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-ticket-sidecar-events.md` (or a `CLAUDE.md` section) that codifies: (1) sidecar events go in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` not `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`; (2) freshness is always computed on read via content-hash + version tags, never stored as a boolean; (3) fail-closed: unreadable state → not-current; (4) latest-by-HLC wins. Reference it from the `TICKET_DIGEST` implementation comments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one log line (or metric increment) on TICKET_DIGEST write success/failure and on digest cache-hit vs stale/miss in the read helper, so enrichment health is observable.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for TICKET_DIGEST write failures or cache-hit/miss events; operators cannot distinguish a missing digest (never written) from a failed write.
findings.location: Scope section / Testing section — no mention of logging, metrics, or tracing for write-path failures
findings.suggested_fix: In the write helper, wrap `stage_and_commit` in a try/except that logs a WARNING on failure (include ticket id and error). In the read helper, emit a DEBUG log or increment a counter distinguishing `digest_cache_hit`, `digest_cache_stale`, and `digest_cache_miss` outcomes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section that `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` normalization produces a string that is then passed to the existing `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` primitive (not a new hash function), explicitly citing `canonical.py` as the single hash seam.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a 'content-hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS) … strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n"' as if this is a new primitive. But `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` is already the project's designated single seam for content-hashing (4+ call-sites confirmed: `conflict_resolver.py`, `mutation.py`, `workflow/schema.py`, `llm/workflow/__init__.py`). The plan's custom normalization (strip + join) is legitimately new logic, but the resulting normalized string should be fed into the existing `canonical.content_hash` rather than a novel hash utility — failing to do so would NIH-rebuild the hash primitive and break the 'one content-hash primitive' invariant documented in `canonical.py`.
findings.location: Scope section: 'A scan/read helper … content_hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS) = title + description + comment bodies, normalized: strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n"'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'content_hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set' with: 'Compute the digest freshness hash by normalizing DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS (strip trailing whitespace per field, join with "\n") into a string, then pass it to the existing `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` — the project's designated single hash seam — rather than introducing a new hash utility.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether the existing `SIGNATURE` event + `attestations[kind]` pattern (`process_signature` → `state['attestations']['ticket-digest']` + `compute_validity`) already satisfies all five ACs before committing to a new `TICKET_DIGEST` event type; if TICKET_DIGEST is genuinely necessary (e.g., because the digest must be reducer-IGNORED and never enter compiled state, which SIGNATURE events do), state that justification explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new TICKET_DIGEST event type without evaluating whether the existing SIGNATURE event + kind-keyed attestations map (process_signature → state['attestations'][kind]) already satisfies all five ACs. That path already provides per-kind last-writer-wins, freshness-on-read (compute_validity), compaction survival, and forward-compat without requiring a new event type, write-allow-list entry, or _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES registration. A new manifest kind (e.g., 'ticket-digest') filed via SIGNATURE could cover the same ground; the plan commits to a novel event type without dismissing this established alternative.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope — 'A new TICKET_DIGEST sidecar event' and edit targets in event_append.py / reducer/_version.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Why not SIGNATURE/attestations?' paragraph to the ## What section. The key distinction is that TICKET_DIGEST is deliberately reducer-IGNORED (never enters compiled state, never snapshotted), whereas SIGNATURE events ARE in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES and ARE snapshotted into compiled state. If this distinction (keeping the digest out of compiled state / hot paths) is the reason for a new sidecar type rather than re-using SIGNATURE, state it explicitly. If compiled-state inclusion is acceptable, use attestations['ticket-digest'] instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that the `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` content-hash computation routes through the existing `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` primitive (not a new hash implementation), and show what field-normalization logic (if any) is new vs. what the existing seam already provides.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'content-hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS = title + description + comment bodies, normalized: strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n")' as if it is a new abstraction to be written. The codebase already has rebar._store.canonical.content_hash as the designated single seam for exactly this purpose (SHA-256 of canonical JSON bytes), used at 4+ call-sites. If the plan intends a new named wrapper function around the existing primitive for field-selection and normalization, it should cite the existing seam explicitly and justify the wrapper; if it intends to write a standalone hash implementation, it is reinventing existing infrastructure.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'A scan/read helper … content_hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'content-hash helper' with: 'call `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash({"title": …, "description": …, "comments": …})` with normalized field values (strip trailing whitespace per field, join comment bodies with "\n") — no new hash primitive, using the existing canonical seam.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
